I'm kind of surprised by `clock()` returning the same value as well.   What happens if the ready queue fills up with a bunch of stuff that takes a bit of time to work through and one of the later tasks calls `clock()`.  It seems that its time value could be skewed in some way.   

I'm thinking that `clock()` should return the most accurate time possible.   Partly because certain parts of curio have been written in a way to allow for periodic heartbeat timers and things like that (e.g., having a task wake up at precise periodic intervals).   It seems that knowing the time as precisely as possible would be essential for that.

> why refit the first level models? shouldn't they be fitted only with k-folds and be used again to predict k-fold-times the predict data?

There are many different flavors of stacking. This implementation is, afaik, how stacking with k-fold cross-validation  is commonly implemented (e.g., see the resource I mentioned in http://rasbt.github.io/mlxtend/user_guide/classifier/StackingCVClassifier/) 

Also, I'd say that it's quite intuitive to refit the 1st level classifiers on the whole dataset as it lowers the (pessimistic) bias and variance of those models (i.e., benefitting from additional data).

I think that both approaches may yield very similar outcomes though, but it might be interesting to see how those two approaches (refitting, not refitting) compare empirically. 

Btw. I found that it was not entirely clear from the article you linked how exactly the stacking was implemented.

Two articles linked in that article though 

- Kaggle ensembling guide (https://mlwave.com/kaggle-ensembling-guide/)

- A Kaggler's Guide to Model Stacking in Practice (http://blog.kaggle.com/2016/12/27/a-kagglers-guide-to-model-stacking-in-practice/)

do refit the first level classifiers as well though:

>
> Let’s say you want to do 2-fold stacking:
>
> 1. Split the train set in 2 parts: train_a and train_b
> 2. Fit a first-stage model on train_a and create predictions for train_b
> 3. Fit the same model on train_b and create predictions for train_a
> 4. Finally fit the model on the entire train set and create predictions for the test set.
> 5. Now train a second-stage stacker model on the probabilities from the first-stage model(s).

(In the excerpt above, it's not clear if the predictions from 4 are also used for fitting in 5 though)

From the other post:

> 3.2 For each base model 
> M1: K-Nearest Neighbors (k = 1) 
> M2: Support Vector Machine (type = 4, cost = 1000)
> 
> 3.2.1 Fit the base model to the training fold and make predictions on the test fold. Store these 
> predictions in train_meta to be used as features for the stacking model
> 
> 4. Fit each base model to the full training dataset and make predictions on the test dataset. Store 
> these predictions inside test_meta
> 5. Fit a new model, S (i.e the stacking model) to train_meta, using M1 and M2 as features. Optionally, include other features from the original training dataset or engineered features
> 6. Use the stacked model S to make final predictions on test_meta

In any case, your suggestion is a good alternative I guess, it's probably less common I'd say and might perform a bit worse but who knows. Probably the only way to find out would be to do an empirical comparison of no one's done that already :)

Phew, that's a tricky one ;). 

Looking at the code, I see that we have sth like

```python
            for key, value in six.iteritems(super(StackingClassifier,
                                            self).get_params(deep=False)):
                if key in ('classifiers', 'meta-classifier'):
                    continue
                else:
                    out['%s' % key] = value

            return out
```

which is basically hiding those two from scikit-learn's grid search. The if clause could be removed to allow tuning the `classifiers` and `meta-classifer` during grid search as well. I just did some experiments, and e.g., the following would work after making that change (note that the `n_neighbors` arguments are provided also if `[clf2, clf3]`, but GridSearchCV` just ignores those in case KNeighborsClassifier (clf1) is not present):

```python
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.naive_bayes import GaussianNB 
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV
from mlxtend.classifier import StackingClassifier

from sklearn import datasets

iris = datasets.load_iris()
X, y = iris.data[:, 1:3], iris.target

# Initializing models

clf1 = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=1)
clf2 = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1)
clf3 = GaussianNB()
lr = LogisticRegression()
sclf = StackingClassifier(classifiers=[clf1, clf2, clf3], 
                          meta_classifier=lr)

params = {'classifiers': [[clf1, clf2, clf3], [clf2, clf3]], 'kneighborsclassifier__n_neighbors': [1, 5]}

grid = GridSearchCV(estimator=sclf, 
                    param_grid=params, 
                    cv=5,
                    refit=True)
grid.fit(X, y)

cv_keys = ('mean_test_score', 'std_test_score', 'params')

for r, _ in enumerate(grid.cv_results_['mean_test_score']):
    print("%0.3f +/- %0.2f %r"
          % (grid.cv_results_[cv_keys[0]][r],
             grid.cv_results_[cv_keys[1]][r] / 2.0,
             grid.cv_results_[cv_keys[2]][r]))

print('Best parameters: %s' % grid.best_params_)
print('Accuracy: %.2f' % grid.best_score_)
```

So, in short, yeah, changing


```python
            for key, value in six.iteritems(super(StackingClassifier,
                                            self).get_params(deep=False)):
                if key in ('classifiers', 'meta-classifier'):
                    continue
                else:
                    out['%s' % key] = value

            return out
```

to 

```python
            for key, value in six.iteritems(super(StackingClassifier,
                                            self).get_params(deep=False)):
                out['%s' % key] = value

            return out
```
would allow that! Happy to make that change (the reason why I included the if-else was that I wasn't sure how it's handled by GridSearchCV, but it seems to be okay :))

@evadot You clearly misread me.

This is not for Windows only. This is an annoyance for **all** operating systems. It just happens to be *slightly* less painful on Linux and OSX. Now you may not care about Windows personally, but alas some people have or want to support all major operating systems, and it would be childish to only care about the ones we prefer (if it were up to me, I'd remove Windows completely from the equation but I can't - even though, my request would still stand).

Many Python libraries took that exact path for the reasons I mentionned. Now, that effectively would make `pysodium` a standalone-library instead of a wrapper. Which may indeed not be desired, and that's ultimately @stef 's decision. Hence my question before putting down all the work.

As I said, I'm completely OK if that ends-up with a no. What I need is a clear stand on this so I can know how to move forward with my other projects that depend on `pysodium`.

Now @stef regarding alternatives, we could imagine having an alternative target install, as `ipython` does using [`extras_requires`](https://wheel.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#defining-conditional-dependencies).

So that if you type:

    pip install pysodium

You end-up with the wrapper, exactly as it is today. **No changes whatsoever.**

And if you type:

    pip install pysodium[standalone]

You end-up with the wrapper installed, and an additional Python package (let's call it - `pysodium-binaries`) that **I would maintain**. When this package is detected by `pysodium`, it shall use it instead of `ctypes` to call the variaous `libsodium` methods. An externally available `libsodium` would not be needed anymore in that case.

I think this approach is better as it keeps a perfect backward compatibility with existing code, adds the new functionality for those who need it while keeping the necessary changes in `pysodium` to a minimum. At the same time, the `pysodium-binaries` library can evolve at its own pace and things are less tightly coupled.

To sum up, the changes needed in `pysodium` would be:
- Update `setup.py` to handle the optional variant.
- Change `pysodium/__init__.py` to detect and use `pysodium-binaries` when available.
- Update the `README.md` file to indicate that a variant is available and why.

Let me know what you think of this. Cheers !

If all of the results need to be inserted to redis, it's recommend to overwrite the `ResultWorker`

```
from pyspider.result import ResultWorker

class MyResultWorker(ResultWorker):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(MyResultWorker, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.redis = redis.StrictRedis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0)

    def on_result(self, task, result):
        data = json.dumps(result ,ensure_ascii=False)
        self.redis.lpush('pyspider_list',  data)
````

and specify the result worker class in the config:

```
{
  ...
  "result_worker": {"result-cls": "my_result_worker.MyResultWorker"},
  ...
}
```

[Sorry for the delay in responding.  I'm in the middle of changing jobs and moving across the continent, so my availability going to be somewhat sporadic.]

Jason, encrypting the entire file is the simplest secure approach, but as you mentioned, "it is the goal of the Crypto keyring to protect the passwords and not other data in the file."  I don't think it's such a great idea to maintain an unencrypted list of servers that a user has access to (OpenSSH hashes its ~/.ssh/known_hosts entries to avoid this) but I'm willing to treat that as a given for now.

The problem with passwords is that they're almost always cryptographically weak (low entropy and frequently reused), so it's really important for any program that handles passwords to leak as little information as possible about them (e.g. their length and their equality with other passwords).   Such a program should also make retrieving password somewhat computationally expensive (e.g. by using something like PBKDF2 with many iterations).

I had actually suggested encrypting all of the *passwords* together, not the entire file.  For example, you could make a JSON array of the passwords, encrypt that, and then put array indexes into the cleartext.

Regarding your points 2, 3, and 5 above, I agree with them in principle, but I think that getting the file format right should trump the other considerations.  For example, if the BasicFileKeyring API does not allow for the file format that we want, then the API---not the file format---should be changed.  Also, avoiding loading all the passwords into memory is nice if possible, but since the master key is already in memory during decryption, it would be wiser to secure the case where an attacker *doesn't* have access to the master key, than to prioritize the case where the attacker has access to the master key but is somehow unable to use it to decrypt the rest of the file.  We should also be careful to avoid making the system more complex just to implement some "nice-to-have" security features that are more likely to contain bugs than they are to prevent a successful attack.

If I were going to implement this for python-keyring, I would probably do something like this:

1. Given the user's master password MP, a random salt MS, and an iteration count i=2000
2. Use PBKDF2-SHA256(MS, MP, i) to generate a 256-bit master key MK
3. Use the master key to derive other keys:
  a. EK = SHA256("EK:" + MK)  # password encryption key
  b. V = SHA256( "V:" + MK)  # verification value
4. Store MS, i, and V as plaintext in the global portion of the file.  This is enough to recover the master key from the master password, and to check whether the correct master password has been entered.
5. For each password:
  a. Generate a random IVp for encrypting the password
  b. Encode the password as <length> + ":" + password, then pad it to a multiple of 64 *bytes*
  c. Encrypt the padded password using EK as the key and IV as the initialization vector (use CBC mode or CTR mode, but definitely not ECB mode), Cp
  d. Base64-encode the IVp and Cp, then store IVp ":" Cp in the individual password field.

Does that make sense?

Hi @pbugnion , the same as for point in the other issue. This is my class to build a figure in gmaps supporting colors and different parameters. The problem is that gmaps only supports featureCollection from geojson and you need to create a new FeatureCollection of each point, line or polygons.

I hope it helps ;)

```python
import json
import geojson
import gmaps
import ipywidgets

class GmapMap:

    def __init__(self):
        self.layers_poly = []
        self.layers_lines = []
        self.layers_points = []
        self.layers = []
        self.fig = None

    def add_point(self, lat, lon, **kwargs):

        # List of kwargs:
            # hover_text = ''
            # fill_color = None
            # fill_opacity = 1.0
            # stroke_color = None
            # stroke_opacity = 1.0
            # scale = 3
            # info_box_content = None
            # display_info_box = None

        # Create Symbol for Point
        # Create json string
        point = geojson.LineString(((lon, lat), (lon, lat)))

        # Create feature
        point_feature = geojson.Feature(geometry=point)

        # Create FeatureCollection
        # gmaps.geojson_layer.InvalidGeoJson: Only FeatureCollection GeoJSON is currently supported
        point_feature_collection = geojson.FeatureCollection([point_feature])

        # Generate JSON string
        json_string = json.loads(geojson.dumps(point_feature_collection, sort_keys=True))

        # Load JSON
        point_json = gmaps.geojson_layer(json_string, **kwargs)

        # Add to list
        self.layers_points.append(point_json)
        #self.layers.append(point_json)

    def add_line(self, lat_init, lon_init, lat_end, lon_end, **kwargs):

        # List of kwargs:
            # fill_color = None
            # fill_opacity = 0.4
            # stroke_color = None
            # stroke_opacity = 0.8
            # stroke_weight = 1.0

        # Create json string
        line = geojson.LineString(((lon_init, lat_init), (lon_end, lat_end)))

        # Create feature
        line_feature = geojson.Feature(geometry=line)

        # Create FeatureCollection
        # gmaps.geojson_layer.InvalidGeoJson: Only FeatureCollection GeoJSON is currently supported
        line_feature_collection = geojson.FeatureCollection([line_feature])

        # Generate JSON string
        json_string = json.loads(geojson.dumps(line_feature_collection, sort_keys=True))

        # Load JSON
        line_json = gmaps.geojson_layer(json_string, **kwargs)

        # Add to list
        self.layers_lines.append(line_json)
        #self.layers.append(line_json)

    def add_polygon(self, poly_list, **kwargs):

        # List of kwargs:
            # fill_color = None
            # fill_opacity = 0.4
            # stroke_color = None
            # stroke_opacity = 0.8
            # stroke_weight = 1.0

        # Create json string
        list_of_polygons = list()
        list_of_polygons.append(tuple(poly_list))

        poly = geojson.Polygon(tuple(list_of_polygons))

        # Create feature
        poly_feature = geojson.Feature(geometry=poly)

        # Create FeatureCollection
        # gmaps.geojson_layer.InvalidGeoJson: Only FeatureCollection GeoJSON is currently supported
        poly_feature_collection = geojson.FeatureCollection([poly_feature])

        # Generate JSON string
        json_string = json.loads(geojson.dumps(poly_feature_collection, sort_keys=False))

        # Load JSON
        poly_json = gmaps.geojson_layer(json_string, **kwargs)

        # Add to list
        self.layers_poly.append(poly_json)
        self.layers.append(poly_json)

    def plot(self, heightpx=750, weightpx=570):

        # Generate figure
        self.fig = gmaps.figure()

        # Configure
        gmaps.configure(api_key=googlekey)


        for layer in self.layers:
            self.fig.add_layer(layer)

        for layer in self.layers_lines:
            self.fig.add_layer(layer)

        for layer in self.layers_points:
            self.fig.add_layer(layer)





        # Change Size
        for k, v in self.fig.widgets.items():
            if isinstance(v, ipywidgets.widgets.widget_layout.Layout):
                v.align_self = None
                if v.height == '400px':
                    v.height = str(heightpx) + 'px'
                    v.width = str(weightpx) + 'px'
                    v.align_self = None
                    v.align_items = 'center'
                    v.justify_content = 'center'
                    v.text_align = 'center'

        return self.fig

Looks like `.scale()` isn't convolution resize method at all and doesn't use filters. From [the docs](http://www.graphicsmagick.org/Magick++/Image.html#scale):

> scale
Resize image by using simple ratio algorithm which provides good quality:

Only `.resize()` and `.zoom()` in earlier versions are convolution-based. Most likely `.scale()` method uses [supersampling resizing](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/12526449/what-is-the-name-of-this-algorithm-and-how-does-it-compare-to-other-image-resam). In support of this theory images upscaled with `.scale()` methods are pixelated like with simple nearest neighbor method. 

I think `.scale()` should be replaced with `.zoom()` (because it presents in every version) in some places:

[pgmagick.api.Image.scale](https://github.com/hhatto/pgmagick/blob/master/pgmagick/api.py#L720)
[example/pgmagick_prof.py](https://github.com/hhatto/pgmagick/blob/49ae1e5d56f52f4dbc93b8b3ba8aedc9618ed7dc/example/pgmagick_prof.py)

That's by design. Windows only lets you query IO counters based on physical disk, not partitions.
That is, if PhysicalDrive0 has two partitions, C:\ and D:\ (as is your case), you cannot filter IO activity only for C:\ or D:\.

And yes, `device` and `mountpoint` on Windows are always the same string.
Maybe we can change that so the instead of `device='C:\\'` you get `device='PhysicalDrive0'`, although I'm not sure whether it's possible.

@crazyglasses Sorry, misread your question.  For docker containers, there isn't a hard distinction between CPU/mem inside or outside the container like there would be on a VM -- all containers share the same kernel and there isn't a namespace for cpus or memory that causes the container to see those differently.  There are cgroups, which you can use to limit resource usage of the containers (e.g. certain cores or period/quota of CPU), but that would just show as the processes in the container using a more limited amount of resources, not change what psutil would report as far as I understand it.  I know `docker stats` will give you what it sounds like you want -- I think it relies on cgroup stats somewhere in the proc filesystem, but I'm not sure if psutil can pick up those or not.

The pertinent question asked above is not really about calling `loop.stop`, but about getting at the heart of how to handle an asyncio based lib (discord.py) in a Thread. We are getting close, as created a loop and setting it in the thread has gotten me to the loop running, but `client.run(...)` blocks, so I cannot close the client through signals generated by the main thread...

`client.run` is blocking. This is intentional, and in single-thread capacity unavoidable because it is required to run the asyncio event loop discord.py runs on.

If you wish to run stuff while the bot is running, you can either push the event loop (or your task) onto another thread or you can create background tasks as mentioned in the [FAQ](http://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/faq.html#how-do-i-run-something-in-the-background).

Additionally, `client.login` does not substitute `client.run`. `client.run` wraps around the coroutine `client.start`, which runs both `client.login` and `client.connect`. If you only run `login`, your bot will not function properly as it won't be able to hear any events.

It's so that I can set `RTV_PAGER` to be something like `mdless` but I don't want to use `mdless` for all `PAGER` duties.

I'd have cited that in my test comment above, but the stock version of `mdless` exits when the page is fully rendered, causing the same problem you get with `rtv` you would get if you set `PAGER=more` instead of `PAGER=less`.  I've made local patch to `mdless` to prevent that from happening, but I'm sure one of the other half-dozen command-line markdown viewers available implements proper `less` behaviour out of the box.  That'll give `rtv` users a nicer overall experience when viewing long comments that include markdown, I think.

There are certainly other ways to accomplish this, and until I wrote up the patch over the weekend, I had been invoking `rtv` like:

```
$ PAGER=mdless rtf
```

Which keeps `PAGER` sane for most stuff and sets it to what I want for this particular command.  I just threw this together after I'd run that from my command history again and thought it might be better to have a convenience variable.

yeah- using the same data for both means that when we fit the gradient-boosted model, we're asking the feature_learning model to make predictions on it's own training data (which it's going to be really good at, of course). the gradient boosted model will then learn "wow, these features from the feature_learning model are really good! i'm gonna rely heavily on those and not the others". 

one of my favorite ways of checking for overfitting is to score the model on both the test data, and the training data, to see if the scores are noticeably different. If the training score is much better, there's probably some overfitting somewhere. 

deep learning in general is prone to overfitting (one of my next steps in auto_ml is to add in more regularization to make it more difficult for them to overfit), particularly on small datasets, so we just have to be extra careful when it's in the mix.

All of the pieces for what you want to do are there, but spread out into layers that include checks for conditions that you are avoiding.  For example, by limiting yourself to ReadProperty, you know that you won't need any of the application layer segmentation state machine, and by being on the same network you won't need any of the topology management of the network layer.

The closest existing code is the `decode_packet()` skipping over the `decode_ethernet()` and `decode_vlan()` part at the top, assuming you are going to almost but not quite have a stack on the `bacpypes.udp` module.  That decoding code goes through each layer, using the results of the previous layer to decode the next layer.  You application will be doing the exact opposite on the request side, starting from the specific request and eventually getting to something you can send out the UDP port (an OriginalUnicastNPDU BVLL message), and very similar on the response side, skipping over incoming packets that are not specifically addressed to you (you don't need to open a socket for broadcast traffic), and those that are intended for your network layer or application layer.

I also *tried* to incorporate `sf_read_raw()` and `sf_write_raw()` in #72, but I didn't find a meaningful way to do this. So I skipped them.

Regarding API, note that the word "raw" is quite ambiguous in the context of libsndfile, and since you are using buffers, there is also a high potential for confusion with `buffer_read()` and `buffer_write()`.

Also note that `sf_read_raw()` and `sf_write_raw()` work only on a subset of the supported file types (I don't know exactly, probably only the RIFF-based types?), therefore I don't think it's worth supporting it.

Did you try using the `wave` and/or `aiff` modules from the standard library?
They are quite limited, but if you need packed 24bit data in memory, they could actually be the right thing to use?

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):

    bus = connect_and_authenticate(session)
  File "/home/dmitry/.local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jeepney/integrate/blocking.py", line 76, in connect_and_authenticate

  File "/home/dmitry/.local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jeepney/auth.py", line 25, in feed
    line, self.buffer = self.buffer.split(b'\r\n', 1)
ValueError: not enough values to unpack (expected 2, got 1)

That is a bug in Jeepney, it can be fixed with [this change](https://gitlab.com/takluyver/jeepney/merge_requests/4/diffs). After fixing it I get:
```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/LaZagne/Linux/lazagne/softwares/wallet/libsecret.py", line 44, in run
    bus = connect_and_authenticate(session)
  File "/home/dmitry/.local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jeepney/integrate/blocking.py", line 78, in connect_and_authenticate
    raise Exception("Authentication failed: %r" % auth_parser.error)
Exception: Authentication failed: b'REJECTED EXTERNAL'
```

That means that D-Bus is rejecting the connection. It is because Jeepney is trying to authenticate as root, not as the original user.

The only way to fix it is monkey-patching Jeepney's `make_auth_external()` function. Try https://github.com/mitya57/LaZagne/commit/06aa6b4f3a9b2a2a79344cf92295c4c86ccfcf64 which should help.

Discussed in #tahoe-lafs and I think what we concluded is this:

 - new clients send ABILITIES (`abilities`, a new message) on connect
 - new clients wait for WELCOME from the server (if it includes the URL, then it is in mitigation mode and demands a sacrifice/token)
 - new clients send PERMISSION (`submit-permission`, a new message) with their token

 - old clients continue to send BIND right away

 - new server waits for some message from a client before sending WELCOME; if this message is ABILITIES it's a new client, and if not it's an old-client.
 - if we're doing mitigation:
    - if it's an old client, send an error in the WELCOME
    - if it's a new client, include the URI in the WELCOME

@warner notes this will be an extra "1 round-trip" delay for new client + new server (because the server waits for the new message from the client, and the client waits for the welcome before sending bind).

Are you using the key `'user_id'` in the session anywhere? If you don't intentionally set `'user_id'` in the session, then maybe clear any cookies on your browser for your app's url in case something old is hanging about. 

looking at the `reload_user()` function, the only way to end up with the "No user_loader..." exception is to have `reload_user` called with `user = None` and `user_id = something other than None`. Then because no user_callback is defined the exception is raised. 

With your workaround, because you do set a user_callback (via user_loader) this suppresses the exception from firing. But as that user_callback returns `None` itself, an anonymous_user is set later anyway and the function completes.

In your suggested solution, the code does check the other callbacks already but it does it in the `_load_user()` function. e.g. [https://github.com/maxcountryman/flask-login/blob/05b8f8602ee81f7d131607ab4757899883462ba6/flask_login/login_manager.py#L312](url)

@bmbouter Thanks for your response. I already described the cons of the setuptools ``install_requires`` mechanism in my previous response, but it seems you missed that, no problem, I'll repeat my point of view about that in more details.

Current version of ``setup.py`` in master branch doesn't have an ``install_requires`` directive because now ``python-redmine`` bundles (vendors) all the dependencies inside itself. Before version 1.4.0 there was an ``install_requires`` directive which specified the minimum required ``python-requests`` version for ``python-redmine`` to work.

``python-requests`` is a very very popular library which is used by A LOT of projects, so, let's imagine that a new version of ``python-requests`` was released and I'll change the ``install_requires`` directive to point to that version, to be sure that when users of ``python-redmine`` will get a new version they will also get a new version of ``python-requests``, so far so good. But as I said ``python-requests`` is a very popular library, so imagine that you also have another library installed that will stop working because of this new version of ``python-requests``, I think you won't be happy because of that. Of course there is a solution to this problem, you'll have to create two separate virtualenvs and put ``python-redmine`` in one of them and your second library in another, but that will require you to do that and to waste your time on that. I don't want to waste the time of users of ``python-redmine`` no matter how good my intentions are.

So, bundling (vendoring) the software (with the automatic fallback to using the newest version of ``python-requests`` if it's available in the system of course, like I described in my proposal in my previous response) is the only way to make the software secure, up-to-date and with a happy user base.

Hi.  Thanks for getting in touch.  I've had a quick think about this and have a couple of ideas...

First off, the sort of tabular display that you're hoping to create is exactly the sort of thing that I was expecting to be handled by the multi-column list widget in https://github.com/peterbrittain/asciimatics/issues/31.  Sadly that doesn't exist yet.  It shouldn't be too hard to create based on the existing ListBox, but I haven't done that yet and don't know when I'll get the time to do so.  In the meantime, you could try creating the new widget yourself (and I'd happily review any pull request), or approximating it by using the ListBox with some hacked formatting as you populate the data.

As for the updates in the background?  I think the issue that you're going to hit there is that my recent changes to reduce CPU usage mean that Frames don't redraw unless they think they need to do so.  You therefore have to force the refresh when your data changes.  Depending on how architectural you want to be, you could add a new method to Frame (e.g. force_update) that forces it to redraw next time asciimatics polls it for updates, or you could just hack frame_update_count to always return 1 - which will redraw the Frame on every refresh and so dynamically update as your model changes, but at the cost of a load of wasted CPU. Hi @HunjaeJung,

One thing about BTYD models is that they tend to treat customers with frequency=0 as non-informative. That is, if we've only seen one that customer one time, it is difficult to infer their average behaviour since we don't have any repeated data points.

That Gamma-Gamma model of monetary value is similar in that it doesn't claim to know the average purchase amount for frequency=0 customers since we've only seen one data point. So instead, the model assigns all those customers' expected average purchase amounts to be the same as the population mean.

So its not that those customers have a greater monetary value, rather, in this model they're just set to have the mean monetary value. We should expect to see a roughly similar number of customers with monetary values even greater than the frequency=0 customers.

There is a bit of discussion about this in http://www.brucehardie.com/notes/025/ : 

> We note that this is the weighted average of the population mean, E(Z), and the 
> observed average transaction value, \bar{z}. As the number of observations (x) 
> used to compute \bar{z} increases, less weight is placed on the population mean 
> and more weight is placed on the customer’s observed average.

We can see this in the function `conditional_expected_average_profit` -- if `frequency=0`, then it returns `population_mean`, but for large `frequency` the `individual_weight` term dominates and the `population_mean` is down-weighted. Intuitively, if we have many observations from an individual customer, we will put more weight on that customers average, otherwise default to the population mean.

As long as you put the ```encoded_token``` kwarg after ```jti``` (and make that a kwarg as well, like you have it in your example), it shouldn't cause any breaking changes for current users. It can currently be called it via ```get_stored_token('abc123')``` or ```get_stored_token(jti=`abc123`)```, and both of those will still work with your proposed changes. This seems fine to me.

As per the name, I think ```get_jti``` is fine. In this case, we are passing in the encoded token directly ex: ```get_jti(encoded_token)``` instead of grabbing it from the flask context stack, so I think that fits the overall naming convention better.

> From what I've seen, there is no need for refresh tokens to be JWT, they could be base64-encoded random strings, as seen on auth0's blog: https://auth0.com/blog/refresh-tokens-what-are-they-and-when-to-use-them/

As I understand it, this would require the user to have state on their server to go this route, as you now need to be able to look which user the refresh base64 encoded string is for. Unfortunately, that is something I don't want to require for using token refreshing in this application.

If there is another way to make this extension work for your use case, I will consider it. One thing to keep in mind is this library really is opinionated in how it works, and that is kinda by design. It grants a lot of power to developers to do complex things involving JWTs easy in the context of this library, but it isn't a general solution for all JWT scenarios (as you have noticed), and it wont always work with other schemes.

I would be more then happy to go over other ideas you might have to get this working for you, but if your end goal is to basically have a flask application that can consume tokens from other providers, it might be simpler for you to use PyJWT directly. It is a really simple api for handling JWTs (without all the extra stuff I have included in this library), and writing a `@jwt_required` decorator that uses it shouldn't be too difficult at all.

Anyways, hope this give you some food for though. Let me know if you have any questions /comments about this. (and enjoy your vacations!) :)

Copying some of the thoughts from https://github.com/dessibelle/sorl-thumbnail-serializer-field/pull/5#issuecomment-261361148, I think you could sum up my thoughts at the moment with:

As I mentioned [here](https://github.com/dessibelle/sorl-thumbnail-serializer-field/pull/3/files#r84162827) `HyperlinkedSorlImageField` attempts to handle `to_representation()` transparently, in the regard that it attempts to find a sorl generated thumbnail and builds the URL from that. In case it fails, it should fallback to the default `rest_framework.serializers.ImageField` behaviour. This PR appears to break that by requiring `request` if I interpret the code correctly. 

In large part I don't really understand why `request` is needed, as explicitly requiring (and raising if it's missing) breaks the fallback to `rest_framework.serializers.ImageField.to_representation()` which doesn't
rely on `request.build_absolute_uri()`. This is by design and something I'm not willing to let go without discussion. The current implementation does in deed try to take advantage of `request` if available, but can still function without it.

The same goes for `url_prefix` kwarg/setting which I'm not convinced is within the scope of sorl-thumbnail-serializer-field. Off the top of my head I'd say URL settings is better handled by other parts of the Django stack, and should ideally just work given that the `ImageField` and its corresponding serialiser is correctly setup. Please add a clear and reproducible description of your use case and solution to the PR description, and I think it'll be clearer what the intention is.

Also, [tests are failing](https://travis-ci.org/dessibelle/sorl-thumbnail-serializer-field/builds/167413208) and should probably be fixed before merging :)

benchmark script:
```python
import mxnet as mx
import sys
import os
import scipy
import numpy as np
from mxnet.test_utils import rand_ndarray, assert_almost_equal
import time

def measure_cost(repeat, a, b, c, out=None):
    # start bench
    start = time.time()
    results = []
    for i in range(repeat):
        results.append(mx.nd.sparse.add_n(a, b, c, out=out))
    for result in results:
        result.wait_to_read()
    end = time.time()
    diff = end - start
    return diff / repeat

def measure_fallback(repeat, a):
    # start bench
    start = time.time()
    results = []
    for i in range(repeat):
        results.append(a.tostype('default'))
    for result in results:
        result.wait_to_read()
    end = time.time()
    diff = end - start
    return diff / repeat

def main():
    shape = (128, 1000000)
    dns = np.random.uniform(size=shape)
    # context = mx.gpu(0)
    context = mx.cpu()
    mx_dns1 = mx.nd.array(dns, ctx=context)
    mx_dns2 = mx.nd.array(dns, ctx=context)
    for density in [0.01, 0.005, 0.001, 0.0005, 0.0001]:
        mx_csr = rand_ndarray(shape=shape, stype='csr', density=density).as_in_context(context)
        mx_csr_dns = mx_csr.tostype('default')
        sparse_cost = 0.0
        dns_cost = 0.0
        mx.nd.waitall()
        #warmup
        check = mx.nd.sparse.add_n(mx_dns1, mx_csr, mx_dns2)
        dns1 = dns + mx_csr_dns.asnumpy() + dns
        assert_almost_equal(check.asnumpy(), dns1, atol=1e-5, rtol=1e-4)
        mx.nd.waitall()
        for i in range(20):
            sparse_cost += measure_cost(5, mx_dns1, mx_csr, mx_dns2)
            dns_cost += measure_cost(5, mx_dns1, mx_csr_dns, mx_dns2)
        print("%.2f %%" % (density*100), dns_cost / sparse_cost)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

Can we close this?

REQ/REP sockets must always follow a recv with a send and vice versa. That's why your example is hanging:

1. `push` sends a message
2. `pull` receives the message
3. `push` will not be able to send until it has received a message.

This artificial limitation is all REQ/REP do on top of ROUTER/DEALER, which is why ROUTER/DEALER are recommended in general to use instead of REQ/REP.

> Also I'm unclear what the role of the Poller is in this example and if it is necessary

It's not strictly necessary in an example this simple. It's mainly there to showing how to use a Poller with asyncio. Usually, you wouldn't use a Poller unless you have multiple sockets on which events may arrive.

Yeah run works by copying the local file to the board and running it, not by running a file already on the board.  If you're curious check the help output to see a good description of what it does (and other commands):
```
Run a script and print its output.
    Run will send the specified file to the board and execute it immediately.
    Any output from the board will be printed to the console (note that this is
    not a 'shell' and you can't send input to the program).
    Note that if your code has a main or infinite loop you should add the --no-output
    option.  This will run the script and immediately exit without waiting for
    the script to finish and print output.
    For example to run a test.py script and print any output after it finishes:
      ampy --port /board/serial/port run test.py
    Or to run test.py and not wait for it to finish:
      ampy --port /board/serial/port run --no-output test.py
```
If you missed it too there's a good guide that describes the usage of the commands here: https://learn.adafruit.com/micropython-basics-load-files-and-run-code/run-code

Ah, this is a case of PGPy not having support for partial packet lengths just yet (see #95), but it is something I am planning to implement for the next release.

Depending on how you invoke gpg, it will chunk the data packets using partial packet length headers so it can stream the encrypt/decrypt operations while still receiving input. That looks to be what has happened here:

```shell

	Pub alg - RSA Encrypt or Sign(pub 1)
	RSA m^e mod n(2046 bits) - ...
		-> m = sym alg(1 byte) + checksum(2 bytes) + PKCS-1 block type 02
New: Symmetrically Encrypted and MDC Packet(tag 18)(4096 bytes) partial start
	Ver 1
	Encrypted data [sym alg is specified in pub-key encrypted session key]
		(plain text + MDC SHA1(20 bytes))
New: 	(86 bytes) partial end

I have seen in your skeleton app that your schemas are not setup correctly. If you want to use a blueprint like that ```Blueprint('api', __name__, url_prefix='/api')``` you must add the api prefix to all your endpoints in your schemas. So ```self_view = 'person_detail'``` becomes ```self_view = 'api.person_detail'```. First update your schemas and tell me if it fix the issue. Not that I'm talking about about the api prefix, the first parameter of your blueprint not the url prefix. For example: ```Blueprint('api_prefix', __name__, url_prefix='/api')``` your schema must looks like this ```self_view = 'api_prefix.person_detail'```.

Actually it's this bug over here: bugs.python.org/issue22107

in `tempfile.py` `TemporaryFile()` will be called. 
This is roughly what you'll find in there:
```
# global constant TMP_MAX
if hasattr(_os, 'TMP_MAX'):
    TMP_MAX = _os.TMP_MAX  # = 2147483647 on my machine
else:
    TMP_MAX = 10000

# in _mkstemp_inner(), called by TemporaryFile
for seq in range(TMP_MAX):
   try:
      os.open(filename+seq)
   except PermissionError:
      if os.is_windows():
         continue
```
Which leads to `tempfile` trying `2147483647`  different filenames before giving up.
A workaround until this is fixed in python and you have the newest version is:
```
import os
os.TMP = 1000  # choose a number reached quicker than 2**31
from comtypes import _check_version; _check_version('')
# now it tries a 1000 times and happily chooses another directory.
```

This isn't an issue with boost 1.60. It's a problem with the naming convention for the boost_python file names. `setup.py` expects the files to be named `libboost_python-py<majorversion><minorversion>` under Linux, which appears to be true for most common Linux distributions. On Arch, this isn't true. Instead, it uses the convention `libboost_python3` for python3 and simply `libboost_python` for python2.

I mentioned OSX because it happens to use the same naming convention as Arch.

What I commented earlier is not really relevant.

The issue is not so much unquoting `%23` but instead not percent-encoding `#` afterwards when re-building the URI. (And maybe `?` should not be there in `_unquotepath` even. The URL being already parsed into parts before unquoting the path, it should be correct to percent-decode all but `%2F` (`/`).)

In RFC 3986, `pchars`, valid characters for each path segment, are:

```
unreserved    = ALPHA / DIGIT / "-" / "." / "_" / "~"
pct-encoded   = "%" HEXDIG HEXDIG
sub-delims    = "!" / "$" / "&" / "'" / "(" / ")"
                 / "*" / "+" / "," / ";" / "="
pchar         = unreserved / pct-encoded / sub-delims / ":" / "@"
```
So, as `/` is used as path segment delimiter, and `%` is there for untouched percent-encoded chars, I believe safe characters (not needing percent-encoding) in the path component are:
```
unreserved / sub-delims / ":" / "@" / "/" / "%"

>>> print(w3lib.url._pchar_safe_chars.decode('ascii'))
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyzABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ0123456789-._~!$&'()*+,;=:@/%
```

`w3lib.url.canonicalize_url()` currently uses these `_safe_chars` for quoting the path:
```
>>> print(w3lib.url._safe_chars.decode('ascii'))
ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz0123456789_.-%;/?:@&=+$|,#-_.!~*'()
```

The difference being:
```
>>> set(w3lib.url._safe_chars.decode('ascii')) - set(w3lib.url._pchar_safe_chars.decode('ascii'))
{'?', '#', '|'}
```

So indeed, `#` should be quoted. `?` is currently "protected" (not-decoded) in `_unquotepath`. And `|` is the subject of #25 and #80.

I may have missed something, and reading RFC 3986 always confuses me at some point or another. So I guess we'll need to work on more tests and clean `canonicalize_url` and `safe_url_string` to match browsers better.
And maybe @kmike 's #25 already does the right thing already :)

Hi @vcatalano,

Unfortunately, to my knowledge there's no way of doing exactly what you're trying to do here given the current state of the bindings. They're written to only have to handle deserialization rather than go the other way around.

One thing that's pretty close is that `StripeObject` has a method called `serialize` which is used to turn a new or updated object into parameters to send back up to the API. Before encoding those parameters look like a basic dictionary. I think the only problematic difference compared to what you're trying to do is that `serialize` tries to be smart about things and only includes fields that have changed since the object is loaded.

We could consider adding an optional parameter to `serialize` along the lines of `force=false` that could be used to serialized all fields regardless of whether they're saved or unsaved. I'll doubt we'd tackle that one, but we'd certainly consider a well-tested patch!

I think we both want things simple as possible, I assume you think the current model is simplest, and I think migration to MVC would make the current functionality simpler.

I think following a MVC pattern not only simpler, it is easier to maintain, easier to support cross platform operation, easier to add new functionality later easier, and easier to improve usability by allowing data defined orders of operation.

I realise that using an MVC pattern is more complex to understand in the abstract, than having a lot of functions which mange state conditionals and state transitions all in the same function and operate in simple loops.

I assert that MVC is more DRY, more testable, more extensible and clearer to read in practice.

                     # "timeOfDay": (21, 9.5)
        },

        "sequence":{"fieldname": u"sequence",
                    "type"     : "SDRCategoryEncoder",
                    "name"     : "sequence",
                    "w"        : _ACTIVE_BITS_ENCODER,
                    "n"        : _WIDTH_ENCODER,
                    "forced"   : True
        }
        # "index" : {"fieldname": u"category",
        #           "type"     : "ScalarEncoder",
        #           "name"     : u"index",
        #           "clipInput": True,
        #           "minval"   : 0.0,
        #           "maxval"   : 10.0,
        #           "w"        : _ACTIVE_BITS_ENCODER,
        #           "n"        : _WIDTH_ENCODER,
        #           "forced"  : True
        #
        # }

    })
    return encoder

def createRecordSensor(network, name, dataSource):
    regionType = "py.RecordSensor"
    regionParams = json.dumps({"verbosity":_VERBOSITY})
    network.addRegion(name, regionType, regionParams)
    sensorRegion = network.regions[name].getSelf()
    sensorRegion.encoder = createEncoder()
    sensorRegion.dataSource = dataSource
    print("Create the encoder successfully!")
    return  sensorRegion

def createSpatialPooler(network, name, inputWidth):
    #Create the spatial pooler region
    SP_PARAMS["inputWidth"] = inputWidth
    spatialPoolerRegion = network.addRegion(name, "py.SPRegion", json.dumps(SP_PARAMS))
    #Make sure the learning is enabled
    spatialPoolerRegion.setParameter("learningMode", True)
    spatialPoolerRegion.setParameter("anomalyMode", False)
    return spatialPoolerRegion

def createTemporalPooler(network, name):
    temporalPoolerRegion = network.addRegion(name, "py.TPRegion", json.dumps(TP_PARAMS))
    temporalPoolerRegion.setParameter("topDownMode", True)
    temporalPoolerRegion.setParameter("learningMode", True)
    temporalPoolerRegion.setParameter("inferenceMode", True)
    temporalPoolerRegion.setParameter("anomalyMode", True)
    return temporalPoolerRegion

def createClassifier(network, name):
    classifier = network.addRegion(name, "py.CLAClassifierRegion", json.dumps(ClASSIFIER_PARAMS))
    classifier.setParameter("inferenceMode", True)
    classifier.setParameter("learningMode", True)
    return classifier

def createNetwork(leftDataSource, rightDataSource):

    network = Network()
#-----------------------------------------FIRST LEVEL---------------------------------------------------
    #Create the left side TM
    LeftSensorRecorder = createRecordSensor(network, name=_L1_LEFT_RECORD_SENSOR, dataSource=leftDataSource)
    createSpatialPooler(network, name=_L1_LEFT_SPATIAL_POOLER, inputWidth=LeftSensorRecorder.encoder.getWidth())
    LeftTemporalPooler = createTemporalPooler(network, name=_L1_LEFT_TEMPORAL_POOLER)
    createClassifier(network, name=_L1_LEFT_CLASSIFIER)

    #Link the components
    network.link(_L1_LEFT_RECORD_SENSOR, _L1_LEFT_SPATIAL_POOLER, _LINK_TYPE, _LINK_PARAMS)
    network.link(_L1_LEFT_SPATIAL_POOLER, _L1_LEFT_TEMPORAL_POOLER, _LINK_TYPE, _LINK_PARAMS)
    network.link(_L1_LEFT_TEMPORAL_POOLER, _L1_LEFT_CLASSIFIER, _LINK_TYPE,_LINK_PARAMS,
                 srcOutput="bottomUpOut", destInput="bottomUpIn")
#********************************************************************************************************
    #IMPORTANT: Define the category source output and the destination category input!!!
    network.link(_L1_LEFT_RECORD_SENSOR, _L1_LEFT_CLASSIFIER, _LINK_TYPE, _LINK_PARAMS,
                 srcOutput="sourceOut", destInput="categoryIn")
#********************************************************************************************************




    #Create the right side TM
    RightSensorRecorder = createRecordSensor(network, name=_L1_RIGHT_RECORD_SENSOR, dataSource=rightDataSource)
    createSpatialPooler(network, name=_L1_RIGHT_SPATIAL_POOLER, inputWidth=LeftSensorRecorder.encoder.getWidth())
    RightTemporalPooler = createTemporalPooler(network, name=_L1_RIGHT_TEMPORAL_POOLER)
    createClassifier(network, name=_L1_RIGHT_CLASSIFIER)

    #Link the components
    network.link(_L1_RIGHT_RECORD_SENSOR, _L1_RIGHT_SPATIAL_POOLER, _LINK_TYPE, _LINK_PARAMS)
    network.link(_L1_RIGHT_SPATIAL_POOLER, _L1_RIGHT_TEMPORAL_POOLER, _LINK_TYPE, _LINK_PARAMS)
    network.link(_L1_RIGHT_TEMPORAL_POOLER, _L1_RIGHT_CLASSIFIER, _LINK_TYPE,_LINK_PARAMS)
    network.link(_L1_RIGHT_RECORD_SENSOR, _L1_RIGHT_CLASSIFIER, _LINK_TYPE, _LINK_PARAMS,
                 srcOutput="sourceOut", destInput="categoryIn")
#
# #-----------------------------------------SECOND LEVEL---------------------------------------------------
    l2inputWidth = LeftTemporalPooler.getSelf().getOutputElementCount("bottomUpOut") + \
                   RightTemporalPooler.getSelf().getOutputElementCount("bottomUpOut")
    createSpatialPooler(network, name=_L2_SPATIAL_POOLER, inputWidth=l2inputWidth)
    createTemporalPooler(network, name=_L2_TEMPORAL_POOLER)
    # This classifier is not necessary
    # createClassifier(network, name=_L2_CLASSIFIER)

    #Link the components
    network.link(_L1_LEFT_TEMPORAL_POOLER, _L2_SPATIAL_POOLER, _LINK_TYPE, _LINK_PARAMS,
                 srcOutput="bottomUpOut", destInput="bottomUpIn")
    network.link(_L1_RIGHT_TEMPORAL_POOLER, _L2_SPATIAL_POOLER, _LINK_TYPE, _LINK_PARAMS,
                 srcOutput="bottomUpOut", destInput="bottomUpIn")


    network.link(_L2_SPATIAL_POOLER, _L2_TEMPORAL_POOLER, _LINK_TYPE, _LINK_PARAMS)
    network.link( _L2_TEMPORAL_POOLER, _L2_SPATIAL_POOLER, _LINK_TYPE, _LINK_PARAMS,
                  srcOutput="topDownOut", destInput="topDownIn")
    # network.link(_L2_TEMPORAL_POOLER, _L2_CLASSIFIER, _LINK_TYPE, _LINK_PARAMS)

    return network

def runClassifier(classifier, sensorRegion, tpRegion, recordNumber):
    actualInput = float(sensorRegion.getOutputData("sourceOut")[1])
    scalarEncoder = sensorRegion.getSelf().encoder.encoders[1][1]
    bucketIndex = scalarEncoder.getBucketIndices(actualInput)[0]
    tpOutput = tpRegion.getOutputData("bottomUpOut").nonzero()[0]
    classDict = {"actValue": actualInput, "bucketIdx":bucketIndex}

    results = classifier.getSelf().customCompute(recordNum=recordNumber,
                                                 patternNZ=tpOutput,
                                                 classification=classDict)

    mostLikelyResult = sorted(zip(results[1], results["actualValues"]))[-1]
    predictionConfidence = mostLikelyResult[0]
    predictedValue = mostLikelyResult[1]
    return actualInput, predictedValue, predictionConfidence

Just to add another 'production system maintainer' opinion:

* I'm a mere mortal too, I'm happy if I get a distilled version of important changes, specially, the separation between features/fixes and backwards compatible/incompatible. That allows me to decide if a package/lib/whatever can be easily upgraded or not.
* But, this must be strongly paired with a semantic versioning schema. That is, make the version number useful, BUMP the major, when there's a non-backwards compatible change, so I can pin to the major version and still get the new features for that version and bugfixes.
* I really try to avoid pulling once a year (that will require a HUGE amount of effort every time you upgrade, and a lot of pain afterwards to fix everything that was broken), instead, my goal is to deploy at least once a week all the minor versions and once a month all the major upgrades (that we want in). That lowers considerably the total amount of work needed, smooths the upgrades, and allows faster and easier track/adaptation/fix of features and bugs. I think that at this point, we have enough examples in the real world that doing smaller more frequent upgrades is way smoother in every way than doing huge, yearly ones.

> And btw, we already have the author's email, which essentially is the same as signed-off-by?

We have been using it slightly differently.  `Signed-off-by` is a
quality stamp, meaning that you stand behind your code. Quoting from [our conventions](http://invenio-software.org/wiki/Tools/Git/Workflow#Remarksoftenmadeduringcodereview):

```
Signed-off-by
    The author says, in essence: "I have carefully implemented the
    feature without any leftover to-be-fixed places, I have run all
    code kwalitee checks and all relevant unit and functional tests,
    and everything is good. To the best of my knowledge, this commit
    is good to go into the fast merge track"
```

> Model the field-dependent optics PSF

This strikes me as potentially a lot of work to get right.  We might be able to get estimates of the mean Zernike coefficients as a function of position on the fov.  If so, then that plus the spider would probably suffice for most purposes here.

```
I think it's possible to get an install from e.g. conda and make play with eups.  The DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH thing from apple is a real pain,  and we should be able to make eups use links instead of DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH.  I went some of the way with a script that builds a soft-link farm based on the environment (hmm, $SCONSUTILS_DIR/bin/makeLinkFarm), and if that gets around the apple issues it'd be easy to add an option to eups to do this directly (it builds the changes to the environment that it knows it needs to make, and then generates the commands on sh/csh but it could easily generate the link commands).

```

@benjello We think subroles should be split into flat roles to avoid nesting. Opinion?

Having said that, I do agree that this solution is too complex, a little too tailored-made, and is going to make new users frown eyebrows.

I see two escapes:

- Make France flatten their roles. Too bad for the modeling aspect. The rule would be: _if you want to have several group entities and make projection from one to another, then yes, practically, you need at least one unique role in your entity_.
- Offer ways to solve the two issues mentioned above without subroles.

The first one is actually easy to solve: just allow this syntax: `famille.parent[0].menage('code_postal', period)`. 

The second is more challenging, but I believe we could do something like:

```py
is_parent = menage.members.role_in(Famille) == Famille.PARENT
is_first_of_role = menage.members.rank_by_role_in(Famille) == 0
is_first_parent = is_parent * is_first_of_role
benefits_menage = menage.sum(benefits_ind * is_first_parent)  
```

(Of couse, `is_first_parent` could be extracted to its own formula)

I estimate making this possible to 1 day of work, plus 0.5 for reviews (I may have already done some experiments that shows there is no big trap).

Now the question is, as usual, is it worth it? I guess it depends how much the italic rule in the beginning of this message feel like a too big constraint for those who model tax and benefit systems

Sorry for the late response. One thing I would like to clarify is that the X-Ray service aggregates metadata and annotations to trace level. In the `aiohttp` case all segments/subsegments generated for a single incoming request are already correlated with a trace id, so as long as you add the `request_id` to any segment/subsegment, you can then filer on that request id and see all segments/subsegments for that request. IMO a recorder level hook is not necessary as the recorder will blindly add the pre-defined metadata/annotations to each trace entity, which increases unnecessary payload and adds more overhead.

Having said that, I do think there should be a more convenient way to add metadata/annotations to X-Ray provided middleware or library patcher, to make user code less verbose. I propose some callback that you register on a per middleware/library basis. In the `aiohttp` case you will do something like

```python
def my_cb(request, entity):
    id = request.headers['x-request-id']
    entity.put_annotation('id', id)
    metadata = generate_whatever_i_need()
    entity.put_metadata(metadata)

xray_recorder.register_callback('aiohttp', my_cb)
```

Then in the X-Ray `aiohttp` middleware it will do some extra work

```python
your_cb = xray_recorder.get_cb('aiohttp')
your_cb(request, segment)
```

This way any `aiohttp` user can register any callback and extract whatever extra information they want and add adds annotations/metadata. We can do similar thing on third party library patcher to have the recorder to do some customized work to add additional info when doing capture work.

But some explicit contract is necessary so the recorder knows what parameter to pass to the user defined callback and the users know what parameters are expected to be provided when writing the callback.

Please let me know your thoughts.

I vote for including them everywhere as access control is done at a low level (in the `Memory.write()`), so the `force` flag would need to travel from our public APIs all the way down to `write`.

I can submit a PR for this tomorrow afternoon.

Hi,

I think this should be treated as an implementation detail of your tearDown method. For example, your TestCase object could have a flag to signal that some particular resource (the VM in your case) should not be destroyed.

There are many ways to pass flags and parameters to TestCase objects, ranging from a custom test runner and/or custom TestSuite constructor, to simple environment variables.

Unfortunately making --failfast unconditionally skip tearDown wouldn't meet all use cases (for example there could be some resources that you do want to destroy and some other that you don't).

One feature that I'd be in favor of, would be an easy way to pass parameters to test cases (e.g. the flag you need), however that's probably not easy to implement within the constraint of the Python unittest stdlib API. Perhaps @rbtcollins has more ideas on this.

To summarize some comments on #5228: my idea for fixing this type of issue once and for all is to use `os.dup2` to begin to replace `sys.{stdout,stderr,stdin}` at the OS level, rather than by mutating the python level handles. That way, `pantsd-runner` will truly operate as if it had been spawned from a CLI, rather than by forking.

I altered `stdio_as` to use `dup` and `dup2`, and that is "working". But the destination fds of `sys.{stdin,stdout,stderr}` that are in use when we actually use `stdio_as` here https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/8c35227c6e3c4a6b85d2f719c9a62031f5e4ca6f/src/python/pants/bin/daemon_pants_runner.py#L120
... are not 0, 1, 2 apparently. I guess during the fork we close stdio, and would adjust stderr/stdout to point to other destinations?

So I think that rather than `stdio_as`, what we might want to do is have the forked `pantsd-runner` explicitly call `dup2` to set 0, 1, 2 to the correct fds for interacting with the socket... perhaps somewhere in either src/python/pants/pantsd/process_manager.py or src/python/pants/bin/daemon_pants_runner.py's `post_fork_child` ?

Hi, if I understand your question you are asking how the commands get sent to Sonos.
The commands sent are UPNP commands over TCP IP. You can send commands manually from tools like UPNP Device Spy. To automate things SoCo is a set of Python scripts which does all the hard work for you. EG: a simple example to pause a Sonos device:

import soco

device = soco.SoCo('192.168.0.72')  #set to the IP of your sonos device

device.pause()

The problem with using PKs is that we're essentially relying on database PKs to generate the kolibri contentnode PKs upon export.

The main problem with that is databases in general do not guarantee any ordering unless you explicitly tell to by using an `ORDER BY` clause. That's not too bad in itself, but think of when we need to do a data migration, like say when we do a huge migration (like what we did when we had to apply a lot of migrations recently). It will be easy to forget adding the `ORDER BY` clause, essentially reordering the nodes you have on the content curation side. When that's exported, that will basically change your channel PKs, rendering all URLs wrong!

We can give you a brief overview of the first steps involved with creating a new sim object, and if you still want to import your own custom object then It would be best to contact us directly so we can give you a much longer, more detailed overview.

Adding more Game Objects to any given scene is an involved process. The API and Unity Editor functions currently don't facilitate users adding in custom objects directly, so you would have to essentially do it from scratch.

First get Unity 2017.1.3 and open up the ai2thor/unity folder. You can navigate to Assets/Scenes and pick out which scene you want to create a new object in, and open that scene up into the editor view.

As for the new object itself, you will need to either make or obtain a Mesh for that object, import that mesh file in, and create a new Game Object with a mesh renderer that uses that mesh. You will additionally need texture files to go with the material on that mesh if you want it to look realistic. From there, you should have a game object with a transform component, and a Mesh renderer with your imported and textured mesh assigned to it. To this object add a rigid body and a box collider.

From here, the way to set up a SimObj varies wildly depending on the type of object you want to make. The best thing to do from this point is to pick a pre-existing simObj and mimic the values that it has if the object is close enough to the new custom object that you have in mind. If your custom object is different in terms of how it should behave (can you pick it up, can you open it, etc) then you will need to dive into the SimObj script itself to set it up.

If you get the object itself setup with the correct scripts, there is another set of steps where you need to edit a number of utility scripts to ensure that your new custom object can report metadata to the end user correctly, and it would be easiest to go through that in more detail separately if you require it.

# >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
class TrackHDFIterable:
# >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
    def __init__(self,h5path=''):
        self.p=h5path
        self.hdf=pd.HDFStore(self.p)
        self.frames=int((len(self.hdf.keys())-3)/2)  # this will vary
for storage setup
        self.lo=2
        self.high=self.frames
        self.current=self.lo

    def __iter__(self):
        return self

    def __next__(self):
        if self.current>self.high:
            raise StopIteration
        else:
            framename='frame/f%09d/data'%int(self.current)
            self.current+=1
            return self.hdf[framename].as_matrix()

cc: @msharahan @jjhelmus  @csoja 

In my view, this is most accurately characterized as an Anaconda problem, not a conda problem. The problem is caused by:

- Giving `pkgs/main` a higher channel priority than `pkgs/free`
- Not building any `nomkl` packages in `pkgs/main`

To understand why, consider what would happen if NumPy 1.14 came out and you built _only_ an MKL version of the package; and suppose someone runs `conda install numpy nomkl`. The _first thing_ conda tries to do is maximize the version of NumPy; that will be 1.14 in this case. The _second_ thing it will try to do is maximize the number of `nomkl` packages it can install. But since there are no `nomkl` versions of NumPy 1.14, it has to fall back to the `mkl` version.

That's what is happening here, only it's caused by channel priority instead of version number. The _first_ thing conda tries to do is maximize the channel priority for NumPy. Since it can find NumPy in `pkgs/main`, that's the one it's going to prefer. The _second_ thing conda tries to do is maximize the number of `nomkl` features. But there is no `nomkl` version of the package in `pkgs/main`, so it can't do anything with that.

This can be solved in several ways:
- Revert the channel priority change that gives each subdir its own channel priority.
- Build new `nomkl` equivalents for all packages like NumPy that have `mkl`/`nomkl`.
- Copy the `nomkl` versions of NumPy et. al. from `pkgs/free` into `pkgs/main`.
And I am _very_ glad that you are learning more about the `conda` internals! We need more folks who can make changes like this.

@Juanlu001 the issue is that conda, although written in python, is python agnostic and generic python manager and such 440 is a python specific thing.

On the specific side of conda:

This works as expected
`conda create --name testenv python=3.6.*`

Same as
`conda create --name testenv python=3.*`

But the double equal sign in conda (`==`) is used to match **an exact** version, so 

This works:

`conda create --name testenv python==3.6.0_0` 

But 

`conda create --name testenv python==3.6.*`

Does not work, cause there is no package version equal to `3.6.*`

With the `az` cli, I went with:

`az network dns record-set create --resource-group="deisonk8srg" --zone-name="plusonetechnology.net" --type="A" --name="*"` 

then add the ipv4-address:

`az network dns record a add --resource-group="deisonk8srg" --zone-name="plusonetechnology.net" --record-set-name="*" --ipv4-address="168.61.71.200"`

The command `az network dns record-set add` does not exist:

```
jims@dockeropolis:~$ az network dns record-set add --help
usage: az network dns record-set [-h] {create,show,update,list,delete} ...
az network dns record-set: error: argument subcommand: invalid choice: 'add' (choose from 'create', 'show', 'update', 'list', 'delete')
```

The `az network dns record-set create` help is completely lacking detail:

```
jims@dockeropolis:~$ az network dns record-set create --type A --help

Command
    az network dns record-set create

Arguments
    --name -n           [Required]: The name of the RecordSet, relative to the name of the zone.

    --ttl                         : Record set TTL (time-to-live).  Default: 3600.

Global Arguments

    --output -o                   : Output format.  Allowed values: json, jsonc, list, table, tsv.
                                    Default: json.
    --query                       : JMESPath query string. See http://jmespath.org/ for more
                                    information and examples.
    --verbose                     : Increase logging verbosity. Use --debug for full debug logs.
```

And you are required to create a `record set` before adding records.  Per `az network dns record add --help`:

```
jims@dockeropolis:~$ az network dns record a add --help

Command
    az network dns record a add

Arguments
    --ipv4-address      [Required]: IPV4 address in string notation.

    --verbose                     : Increase logging verbosity. Use --debug for full debug logs.
```

Try and add a record w/o a record set:

jims@dockeropolis:~$ az network dns record a add --resource-group="deisonk8srg" --zone-name="plusonetechnology.net" --record-set-name="bar" --ipv4-address="10.211.55.1"

Here's the conundrum: these commands are all related to replication, so `sql db replica` makes sense as a subgroup of related functionality. However a replica is itself a full database, there is no such ARM resource as a 'replica'. so arguably the noun should be just `sql db`. Both options make sense, I'm happy to hear arguments one way or the other.

I personally don't like putting `link` commands into their own subgroup because that feel like too much hierarchical depth - it forces me as a user to navigate with `-h` too many times to find what I want. If the CLI made it easier to view a command hierarchy this wouldn't bee to bad, but even then I think a subgroup with only 1 command in it feels like we overdid it. Your thoughts?

```
        ....
        from subprocess import STDOUT

        # Launch the Java gateway.
        # We open a pipe to stdin so that the Java gateway can die when the pipe is broken
        if not on_windows:
            # Don't send ctrl-c / SIGINT to the Java gateway:
            def preexec_func():
                signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, signal.SIG_IGN)
            proc = Popen(command, stdin=PIPE, stdout=PIPE, stderr=STDOUT, preexec_fn=preexec_func, env=env)
        else:
            # preexec_fn not supported on Windows
            proc = Popen(command, stdin=PIPE, stdout=PIPE, stderr=STDOUT, env=env)

        gateway_port = None
        # We use select() here in order to avoid blocking indefinitely if the subprocess dies
        # before connecting
        print(command, env)

        while gateway_port is None and proc.poll() is None:
            timeout = 1  # (seconds)
            readable, _, _ = select.select([callback_socket], [], [], timeout)
            if callback_socket in readable:
                gateway_connection = callback_socket.accept()[0]
                # Determine which ephemeral port the server started on:
                gateway_port = read_int(gateway_connection.makefile(mode="rb"))
                gateway_connection.close()
                callback_socket.close()
        if gateway_port is None:
            debug = proc.communicate()
            raise Exception("Java gateway process exited before sending the driver its port number", debug)
        ....
```

Hmm. Let's talk about this.

If this was a bug, it wasn't the fault of `IntType`, so the patch only fixed a very special case.

By design, validator functions are supposed to be able to rely on the value *already being of the correct type*. This is because model validation always runs `field.to_native()` before `validate()`.

In other words, validating *a model* with an `IntType` field containing `"foo"` would have surely raised an error.

If you run `field.validate()` directly, you can get all kinds of weird results:

```python
>>> IntType(min_value=100).validate("1")
>>>
```

Basically, validators are simply not meant to be called directly unless you make sure the type is right.

The way to fix this is to take the `to_native()` call out of the field converter function defined in `validate.py` and put it in `BaseType.validate()` where it will be called regardless of who's invoking the validation. (Note that even this doesn't help if you call a specific validator such as `validate_choices()` directly.)

Can anyone think of a reason why `BaseType.validate()` should *not* call `to_native()` first?

A rule of thumb is that the result of `grad(*args, argnum)` will always have the same shape as `args[argnum]`, i.e. it will always have the same shape as the element that you've differentiated w.r.t.

When you do
```python
fgrad(0.0, x)
```
you're differentiating w.r.t. `0.0`, so the returned value will be a float. When you call
```python
fgrad(x, 0.0)
```
you're differentiating w.r.t. `x`, which has shape `(11,)`, thus the returned array has that shape too.

Hi @gaurav36, thanks for filing this issue. To debug this, first verify that your ``pykmip.conf`` file is configured correctly and is pointing to the right certificate and key files for the client and CA. See [here](https://pykmip.readthedocs.io/en/latest/client.html#configuration) for more information.

Second, verify that your ``server.conf`` file is configured correctly and is pointing to the right certificate and key files for the server and CA. See [here](https://pykmip.readthedocs.io/en/latest/server.html#configuration) for more information.

Third, verify that your client certificate was correctly signed by your CA certificate. You should be able to use the following command to do so:

```
$ openssl verify -CAfile <path/to/your/CA/certificate> <path/to/your/client/certificate>
```

If all three of these steps are verified, you should be able to connect the client and server. If you still have problems, post the errors you are seeing and we will continue from there.

This isn't a breaking change as in such implementation `create_missing()` will accept only those instance attributes, which are used in our bogust managed host creation flow and will raise an exception as before in case anything unexpected was passed (assuming if user passed some "extra" attributes, he uses different creation flow and needs different dependencies).

However, I'm thinking about complete removal of attributes check - there're a lot of flags for host which are harmless and could be used in our flow without any regression. For example, `enabled` flag - right now to set `enabled=False` user needs to do the following:
```python
host = entities.Host()
host.create_missing()
host.enabled = False
host = host.create(create_missing=False)
```
But this flag is not affecting our creation flow so it could be whitelisted to simplify it to `host = entities.Host(enabled=False).create()`. And there's a lot of such "harmless" attributes like host_parameters, ip, root_pass etc.
And even if something from different creation flows was passed (like host collection) - we still won't introduce any regression, as `create_missing()` is raising an exception in such cases, meaning the tests with such flows are definitely creating the host with `create_missing` attribute set to `False`.
Please share your thoughts on whether that attributes check should remain or can be dropped.

```sh
case "$arg" in  # (

    VERBOSE=1;; # (

From sqlite documentation:
> When a database is accessed by multiple connections, and one of the processes modifies the database, the SQLite database is locked until that transaction is committed. The timeout parameter specifies how long the connection should wait for the lock to go away until raising an exception. The default for the timeout parameter is 5.0 (five seconds).

It would seem that your storage device has been unresponsive (likely because it had a long queue of operations done by 200 threads). I would suggest that you symlink `~/.b2_account_info` onto a filesystem which is kept on a different device than the one which is being synced or decrease the amount of threads.

As for the error itself, I have located it and began working on a workaround, but it is not clear for me what should happen when such situation occurs.

What is the behavior you'd expect when the hdd which stores `~/.b2_account_info` is severely overloaded?

@AndrGolubkov There is currently no special support for this. What you can do is simply add more classes with the appropriate sub-components. For instance, you can place an additional box over the fingertip and make that a separate class. However, the algorithm will not be aware of the hierarchical relationship, so it won't tell you that a hand instance is a parent of a particular fingertip instance. You would have to add this kind of reasoning yourself based on the raw predictions (possibly looking at how close they are spatially, if they have consistent size, etc.).

I hope that gives you an idea of how it can be done at least!

I really hate to turn down such a well written pull request, but I'm just not sure this belongs in Tastypie. It seems like a hack around the limitations of Angular and goes against the RESTful best practice of referring to resources by their URIs. Right now I'm really trying to clean up actual bugs and make Tastypie more standards compliant and more maintainable. Wouldn't adding compatibility code to your client be a better solution?

Tastypie also already supplies multiple ways to change data input and output:
1. Custom fields like you're using
2. Tell ToOneField to use a Resource you've defined that only has a 'pk' field defined
3. Use alter_deserialized_detail_data and alter_detail_data_to_serialize to rewrite {"pk": 1} to a URI and a URI back to a dict.

Soon I also plan on adding in a hook that will let you more easily choose a Field class to use when automatically adding introspected fields to a resource.

1. Many of those racking parameters could be useful for (unimplemented) features for standard PV systems.

My personal preference for tests is that they are easy to maintain and understand when they break. Because often someone who didn't write a particular test has to be able to quickly understand why it broke. Too much magic and complex DRY patterns can make that more consuming than the benefit that a bit of DRY can add in the initial authoring phases.
