I think VEX is putting that conditional exit there out of an abundance of caution, as pushes can be conditional in thumb mode. Breaking up blocks on every conditional exit is, as I understand it, necessary for the static analysis to work well (but that's more of an @ltfish question). You can try to get pyvex to optimize it away by using optimized IR (setting the `OPTIMIZE_IR` option in `state.options`, but if it's really a conditional push and you really want the block not to be ended there, you'll need to add such an option into pyvex.

It's hitting claripy, but that doesn't mean that it's hitting symbolic
evaluations. Claripy provides an abstraction layer over different data
domains, so it's used for symbolic values, concrete values, and abstract
values (i.e., strided intervals for VSA). In this case, you're essentially
running into a situation where the backend that handles the abstract value
(in this case, the VSA clarpy backend, handling value sets) can't handle
what is being asked of it (mod), so this sorta cryptic error gets thrown.

Using the `basic` data was a great idea. I had forgotten about that data.

Rob and I talked about how we might have plugins for things like this, and below is what we came up with.

At the same level as `core/`, `datasources/` and `data.py` would be another directory called `plugins/`. The structure of the `plugins` directory would mirror Cass's directory structure, so inside plugins we would have `datasources/ddragon.py` (and `datasources/riotapi/staticdata.py` for the static data pulled from Riot).

Then inside each `cassiopeia/plugins/datasources/....py` file would be methods that monkey patch the methods in `cassiopeia/datasources/....py`. These functions will likely look quite a bit like decorators. Below is an example:

In the file `cassiopeia/plugins/datasources/ddragon.py`:

    from ...datasources/ddragon import DDragonDataSource

    @functools.wraps(DDragonDataSource.get_rune_list)
    def add_default_rune_data(self, query: MutableMapping[str, Any], context: PipelineContext = None):  # copy the signature of wrapped method
        result = DDragonDataSource.get_rune_list(self, query, context)
        # add the defaults to the result
        return result

    DDragonDataSource.get_rune_list = add_default_rune_data

Unfortunately, since the default values for stats and other things are not returned by Riot in their endpoints (i.e. the there is no `basic` data when we hit Riot), these values will have to be hard coded in those cases.

This functionality should work, though. There might be some issues with setting `DDragonDataSource.get_rune_list = add_default_rune_data` depending on how the @register function in the datasources works, however. I didn't dig into that.

import ssl

I don't see a PV-Doc2Vec feature in fastText, currently. Back at Google Mikolov once offered a [quick-and-minimal patch](https://groups.google.com/d/msg/word2vec-toolkit/Q49FIrNOQRo/J6KG8mUj45sJ) to word2vec.c that enabled PV by treating the first token in a sentence as the special paragraph-vector. A similarly minimal bolt-on seems possible here, but if the feature were considered important more capabilities might be desirable – allowing multiple such tags per text, or storing them separately. (Doc2Vec in gensim does these.) And as @amirothman implies, you'd likely want to exempt such symbolic tags from fastText's subword-composition.

> Thus you either need to pay attention to the order of passed numpy arrays (i.e. creating with order='F'), or change Eigen types from MatrixXd to Matrix<double, Dynamic, Dynamic, RowMajor>.

This is an a very important point! End user is likely to have no idea about different storage order so the converter should provide the most logical and expected behavior. At the same time signatures of exposed C++ functions should not change just because we are making bindings for them. 
So, I think the best default behavior is:

- When returning Eigen matrices to create numpy array where first index is col of Eigen matrix (referencing the same data is possible).
- When getting numpy arguments expect that in numpy array first index is column number.

That's a good point.  I think, given that this is already going to unavoidably break backwards compatibility, we might as well go all the way.  Promotion to unsigned values probably won't break anything for addition and subtraction, but will definitely break multiplication or division, such as:

```C++
auto itemstride = a.strides()[0] / a.itemsize(); // With -16/8 I get itemstride = 536870910
```

It is also going to *reduce* compatibility issues in at least some cases by avoiding compiler warnings in calling code for mixed arithmetic.

The way we may changes to `{certbot,letsencrypt}-auto` is kind of complicated/convoluted. Changes to `certbot-auto` and `letsencrypt-auto` in the root directory should only happen during releases (see #3337). The way to modify files in `letsencrypt-auto-source` is to not modify `letsencrypt-auto-source/letsencrypt-auto` directly, but to modify the other files contained in that directory and run `build.py` which will combine all the individual pieces to create `letsencrypt-auto-source/letsencrypt-auto`.

To fix this PR, you should revert the changes to `{certbot,letsencrypt}-auto` in the root. Additionally, if you want to remove the unnecessary whitespace you found, you should modify `letsencrypt-auto-source/pieces/fetch.py`. After doing that, run `build.py` and update the PR containing the resulting changes to all three files.

In order to get certbot to run, I was forced to extend this solution by installing a newer Python and utilizing it, thusly:

```
sudo rm -rf /opt/eff.org/*
sudo yum -y install python36 python36-pip python36-libs python36-tools python36-virtualenv
sudo /usr/bin/pip-3.6 install -U certbot
sudo /usr/bin/pip-3.6 install certbot-apache
sudo /usr/local/bin/certbot renew --debug
```

There's nothing which Streamlink can do in order to fix your issue. Streamlink just concatenates the MPEG-TS segments of the HLS stream provided by Twitch. How the video is being decoded and rendered entirely depends on the implementation of your chosen player and its used decoders.

> About 10-20 seconds later, video frame will change and freeze again

Video encoders for mobile streams usually set different encoding parameters for the stream, because it's a different and more difficult environment. What you're describing sounds like a video keyframe decoding issue.

If one of your players is having decoding issues, then you need to make sure that it is working correctly on its own. VLC comes with is own built-in decoders, so that may be the reason why you're seeing different results on your system when using it instead of MPC-HC, which is using your system's codecs and their configuration.

> I ran the command but can't find the log file in installation or appdata. Where is it?

I'm confused... Did you by any chance use the `--loglevel debug` parameter on the Twitch GUI (you mentioned you were using it)? In either case, the log output is directly being displayed in your terminal. If you were using the Twitch GUI's log, it is useless and unrelated here (this is the Streamlink issue tracker).

However, I don't think that the log output won't help here anyway, since you were saying that audio is working fine, which means that you didn't encounter any stream download interrupts.

https://streamlink.github.io/cli.html

```bash
streamlink --loglevel debug URL QUALITY
```

@shreyamdg I must say, I never had to deal with `pip` behind a proxy. From what I found you could try to set the env variable `HTTP_PROXY` or `HTTPS_PROXY`

```bash
$ export HTTP_PROXY=http://proxy.server.com:8080  # for http proxy, otherwise use HTTPS_PROXY
$ pip install -e .  # in the 'pyecore' repository
```
If this solution works for you, you could write the `HTTP_PROXY=...` into your `.bashrc` (or the equivalent that exists in the linux subsystem in window if that's what you are using).

I also found more information about pypi behind a proxy [here](https://gist.github.com/jtrive84/48f0f8d3ca24db053b0daf4142fc8f8a), I hope this could help you.

I'm a bit confused. What is the relationship between version like "1.1.1" and like "7"? Are they independent?

Also, there seems to be dependency cycle between version bump and new op. If we increase the version, then we need new model zoo to pass tests. But without new op set, we can not have a model zoo on this set... So at some point we have to fail tests... 

I would suggest to do the following:
1. The rule is: new op must always be in a new version
1. postpone the version bump until release
1. Add a macro indicating whether the build is develop or product
1. If the build is develop, allow the version to be 1 larger than the upper bound.

This can be done by changing the following line:
https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/7d1e102e73ffd0479a87d62c8aa950e03cfdda95/onnx/defs/schema.h#L560
```C++
#ifdef DEVELOP
if (!(lower_bound_incl <= ver && upper_bound_incl + 1 >= ver)) { 
#else
if (!(lower_bound_incl <= ver && upper_bound_incl >= ver)) { 
#endif
```

hi,

Most of the problems reported here are due to a broken installation of pyomo. And yours looks like that, too.

Please check that you are able to complete a minimal pyomo optimisation problem with your python installation first:
```python
import pyomo.environ as pe
m = pe.ConcreteModel()
m.x = pe.Var()
m.c = pe.Constraint(expr=m.x >= 1)
m.obj = pe.Objective(expr=m.x)
opt = pe.SolverFactory('glpk')
results = opt.solve(m)
assert results['Solver'][0]['Termination condition'].key == 'optimal'
assert pe.value(m.x) == 1
```

If you get an error there, fix your installation according to the [pyomo documentation](http://www.pyomo.org/documentation/) or look there for help.

Only if that is not the case, reopen this issue here. Make sure to include a complete stack trace, i.e. the full error report for a minimal example!

Closing, since it's most likely a pyomo only issue!
